stem: The nurse is caring for a 78-year-old male client with limited mobility who has developed a new area of skin breakdown. After reviewing the client's electronic health record, complete the bowtie by selecting the appropriate actions, the potential condition, and the parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: Clinical Feed (SBAR)
tabs.content:
Situation: This is the 15:00 SBAR for the 78-year-old male in room 402, who has developed a new Stage I pressure ulcer on his coccyx.
Background: He has a history of stroke with limited mobility. At 09:00, a 2 cm x 2 cm area of non-blanchable erythema with intact skin was noted. A hydrocolloid dressing was applied per protocol.
Assessment: Vitals are stable and he denies pain. Labs are unremarkable, with prealbumin at 16 mg/dL. The client is alert and oriented.
Recommendation: Continue repositioning every 2 hours. Monitor the site for any changes and encourage adequate nutrition and hydration.

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content: General: Alert and oriented x3. Skin: 2 cm x 2 cm area of non-blanchable erythema on coccyx with intact skin. No drainage or odor noted. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, normoactive bowel sounds. Neurological: Gross motor and sensory intact.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content:
• Assess pressure ulcer daily for changes in size, color, and drainage.
  • Apply hydrocolloid dressing to coccyx pressure ulcer per wound care protocol.
  • Reposition patient every 2 hours.
  • Encourage adequate hydration and nutrition.
  • Consult with wound care specialist.

actions.text: Elevate the head of the bed to 45 degrees

hoverRationales.rationale: Correct. Relieving pressure on the coccyx restores blood flow and oxygenation to the ischemic tissue. This is the primary intervention to prevent the progression of a Stage I pressure ulcer and promote healing.
Correct. A hydrocolloid dressing protects the intact epidermis from friction and shear. It creates a moist, occlusive environment that supports healing of the underlying compromised tissue.

Incorrect. Debridement is the removal of necrotic tissue. This client's skin is intact with non-blanchable erythema, indicating no necrosis is present. Performing debridement would cause a break in the skin and increase infection risk.

Incorrect. Elevating the head of the bed to 45 degrees increases shearing forces and direct pressure on the coccyx and sacrum. This action would counteract pressure redistribution efforts and likely worsen the tissue injury.

Incorrect. A deep tissue injury presents as a purple or maroon area of discoloration or a blood-filled blister. This client's assessment describes non-blanchable erythema (redness), which is inconsistent with a DTI.

Correct. The client's physical exam reveals a localized area of intact skin with non-blanchable erythema over a bony prominence (coccyx). This is the classic clinical presentation of a Stage I pressure ulcer.

Incorrect. A Stage II pressure ulcer involves partial-thickness skin loss, presenting as a blister or shallow crater with an exposed dermis. This client's skin is documented as being intact, which rules out this diagnosis.

Incorrect. An unstageable ulcer is a full-thickness wound where the base is obscured by slough or eschar. This client's skin is intact, and the wound base is not applicable, making this diagnosis clinically incorrect.

Correct. Tracking the wound's dimensions, color, and skin integrity provides objective data to evaluate the effectiveness of interventions. Worsening characteristics, such as increased size or skin breakdown, indicate progression of tissue ischemia.

Correct. Pain is a subjective indicator of tissue injury and inflammation. An increase in pain can signal worsening tissue damage or the onset of a complication, such as infection, even before visible changes occur.

Incorrect. While important for long-term healing, serum albumin has a long half-life and reflects nutritional status over weeks, not daily changes. Monitoring this is not a priority for tracking the acute status of a Stage I ulcer.

Incorrect. While adequate hydration is important for tissue perfusion, urine output is a general indicator of fluid status and renal function. It is not a specific or direct parameter for monitoring a localized Stage I pressure ulcer.

topicTags: Pressure Ulcer Staging and Wound Care
